questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the definition of heat transfer?

options.text: The transfer of energy that occurs as a result of a temperature difference
The movement of mass between systems
The conversion of work to heat only
The storage of thermal energy in a substance

questions.explanation: Heat transfer is the transfer of energy due to a temperature difference, from higher to lower temperature.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three basic modes of heat transfer?

options.text: Conduction
Convection
Radiation
Evaporation

questions.explanation: The three basic modes are conduction, convection, and radiation. Evaporation is a phase change process, not a mode of heat transfer.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is specific heat capacity (cp)?

options.text: The heat required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of a substance by one degree
The total heat capacity of a system
The heat required to change phase without temperature change
The ratio of heat to work in a cycle

questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity cp is the heat required to raise the temperature of one unit mass by one degree Fahrenheit or Celsius.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: In conduction heat transfer through a slab, what happens to the heat transfer rate if the material thickness (x) is doubled?

options.text: The rate doubles
The rate is halved
The rate remains the same
The rate quadruples

questions.explanation: From Equation 5: Q = kAΔT / x. Doubling thickness x halves the heat transfer rate.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: Which material has the highest thermal conductivity from the typical values table?

options.text: Copper
Iron
Stainless Steel
Air

questions.explanation: Copper has the highest thermal conductivity at 133.0 Btu/hr-ft-°F, making it an excellent conductor.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the overall heat transfer coefficient (Uo) in a heat exchanger?

options.text: The reciprocal of the total thermal resistance for all layers (tube wall and boundary layers)
The thermal conductivity of the tube material only
The convection coefficient of the hot fluid only
The sum of all individual heat transfer coefficients

questions.explanation: Uo is the overall coefficient accounting for conduction through the tube wall and convection in both boundary layers, as the reciprocal of total resistance.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: How does fouling affect the overall heat transfer coefficient Uo?

options.text: It increases Uo by adding a conductive layer
It decreases Uo by adding an additional layer with its own thermal resistance
It has no effect on Uo
It only affects the fluid velocities

questions.explanation: Fouling adds an extra layer with its own U, increasing total resistance and thus decreasing the overall Uo, sometimes dramatically.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: Why is radiation heat transfer not a workable mode in a nuclear power plant during normal operation?

options.text: It requires extremely high temperature differences to transfer significant heat
It cannot occur in the presence of water
It is too efficient and would overheat the core
It violates the First Law of Thermodynamics

questions.explanation: Radiation requires very large ΔT for meaningful heat transfer due to the T^4 dependence, making it impractical for normal operation.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is core thermal power (CTP)?

options.text: The measure of heat input per unit time from the core to the reactor coolant system
The electrical power output of the generator
The total heat rejected in the condenser
The power consumed by the reactor coolant pumps

questions.explanation: CTP is the rate of heat transfer from the core into the RCS, calculated via heat balance.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: Which equation is used for reactor coolant system heat balance to calculate CTP?

options.text: Q = m × cp × ΔT
Q = m × Δh
Q = Uo × Ao × ΔTo
Q = m × v × A

questions.explanation: For RCS heat balance with no phase change: Q = m × cp × ΔT, where ΔT is TH - TC.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: In a secondary system heat balance, why is the change in enthalpy (Δh) used instead of cp × ΔT?

options.text: Because boiling occurs on the secondary side, requiring both sensible and latent heat accounting
Because cp is not constant for steam
Because there is no temperature change in the steam generator
Because feedwater flow is not measured

questions.explanation: Secondary side involves phase change (sensible + latent), so total enthalpy change Δh = hSteam - hFeedwater is used.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What two methods are commonly used to calculate core thermal power?

options.text: RCS heat balance using m × cp × ΔT and secondary heat balance using m × Δh
Direct neutron flux measurement and electrical output measurement
Condenser heat rejection and pump heat input
Steam flow and feedwater temperature only

questions.explanation: Two methods: RCS heat balance (Q = m cp ΔT) and secondary heat balance (Q = m Δh).

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is percent reactor power?

options.text: Actual core thermal power divided by rated core thermal power
Electrical output divided by thermal input
Steam flow divided by design steam flow
Neutron flux indication only

questions.explanation: Percent reactor power = CTP actual / CTP rated × 100%. It is what nuclear instrumentation indicates.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: From the fuel centerline to the coolant, what is the approximate temperature drop across the helium-filled gap?

options.text: About 200°F
About 50°F
About 1000°F
About 150°F

questions.explanation: Gap temperature drop ~200°F due to helium's relatively low thermal conductivity compared to solids.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the primary mode of heat transfer from the fuel pellet centerline to the outer edge of the pellet?

options.text: Conduction
Convection
Radiation
Evaporation

questions.explanation: Through the solid ceramic pellet, heat transfers by conduction, with a large ~1000°F drop due to low k of UO2.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the zirc-water reaction and at what temperature does it become self-sustaining?

options.text: Rapid oxidation of zirconium with steam producing heat and hydrogen; becomes self-sustaining at ~2200°F
A cooling process that prevents fuel melting
A corrosion reaction that starts at 5000°F
A phase change in the cladding material

questions.explanation: Zirc-water reaction begins ~1800°F and becomes self-sustaining ~2200°F, producing heat and hydrogen that can damage cladding.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the purpose of the overall heat transfer coefficient monitoring in heat exchangers?

options.text: To detect early degradation due to fouling or corrosion
To measure fluid velocities directly
To calculate pump power requirements
To determine condenser vacuum

questions.explanation: Monitoring Uo allows early detection of fouling or corrosion that reduces heat transfer capability.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: In combined conduction and convection heat transfer in a heat exchanger, heat flows through which sequence of layers?

options.text: Convection from hot fluid to tube, conduction through tube wall, convection from tube to cold fluid
Conduction only through all layers
Radiation through the tube wall
Convection from cold to hot fluid only

questions.explanation: Sequence: hot fluid convection → tube wall conduction → cold fluid convection.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What effect does nucleate boiling have on the film convection heat transfer coefficient hf?

options.text: It increases hf by creating turbulence that thins the boundary layer
It decreases hf by adding insulation
It has no effect on hf
It only affects radiation heat transfer

questions.explanation: Nucleate boiling creates bubble motion that thins the boundary layer and increases hf, improving heat transfer.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the primary reason forced convection is used in nuclear power plants instead of natural convection?

options.text: It is more efficient and easier to control the flow rate
Natural convection cannot occur in water
Forced convection requires no pumps
Natural convection produces higher temperatures

questions.explanation: Forced convection via pumps is more efficient and allows precise flow control for heat transfer management.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: According to the example, what is the approximate temperature drop from fuel pellet centerline to outer edge?

options.text: About 1000°F
About 200°F
About 150°F
About 50°F

questions.explanation: Large ~1000°F drop across the pellet due to low thermal conductivity of ceramic UO2.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What happens to cladding integrity if departure from nucleate boiling (DNB) begins?

options.text: Cladding surface temperature rises sharply, potentially initiating zirc-water reaction
Heat transfer improves dramatically
Cladding temperature drops
No effect on cladding

questions.explanation: DNB causes sharp rise in cladding surface temperature, risking zirc-water reaction and cladding damage.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is the function of a heat exchanger in a power plant?

options.text: To remove heat from or add heat to a working fluid without mixing the fluids
To generate electrical power directly
To increase the pressure of the working fluid
To measure fluid flow rate

questions.explanation: Heat exchangers transfer heat between two fluids without mixing them, used for heating, cooling, or steam generation.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: In the heat exchanger performance equation for single-phase fluids, Q = m × cp × ΔT, what does a negative Q indicate?

options.text: Heat is being lost by that fluid
Heat is being gained by that fluid
No heat transfer is occurring
The fluid is changing phase

questions.explanation: Negative Q means heat loss from the fluid; positive means heat gain. The other fluid has the opposite sign.

questions.subject: TH07B
questions.text: What is one example of conduction and one example of convection in a nuclear power plant?

options.text: Conduction: heat transfer across heat exchanger tubes; Convection: heat transfer from fuel rod surface to coolant
Conduction: steam flow in pipes; Convection: through fuel pellet
Both are the same process
Conduction only in condenser, convection only in core

questions.explanation: Conduction through solid tube walls; convection from rod surface into flowing coolant.
